step.title: Step 14
step.decisionPreview: predict(expect_change=True, note='LEFT realigns with target B')

sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: RIGHT.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 17, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental. REQUIRED: call `predict(expect_change=... )` (or `predict(expected_grid=...)`) before each `action(...)`; then read `last_prediction_result`/`prediction_stats`. A `surprise` marks where your world model is wrong -- re-probe there instead of pressing on.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: SPACE didn't shoot the projectile or destroy the frame. Board changed but objects stayed in same positions. Blue projectile at rows 33-34 blocks upward movement. Need to find a way around.
- Plan: Test if blue projectile follows player by moving RIGHT. If it stays, I can navigate around it.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 16 of about 71 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
The blue projectile DID follow the player! Before: player at cols 16-19, projectile at cols 17-18. After RIGHT: player at cols 20-23, projectile at cols 21-22. So the projectile is always centered above the player.
This means I can't escape the projectile by moving horizontally. It always stays 4 rows above the player, centered on the player's column.
So the projectile is essentially attached to the player. This changes my understanding of the game.
Now, the question is: how do I destroy target B? The projectile blocks upward movement, and SPACE doesn't seem to work from a distance.
Let me think about this differently. Maybe I need to:
1. Move the projectile INTO the target frame, which would destroy it?
2. Or maybe I need to get the player closer to the target frame so SPACE works?
But the projectile is always 4 rows above the player. Target B frame is at rows 28-31. If I want the player to be at rows 32-34 (just below the frame), the projectile would be at rows 28-30, which is INSIDE the frame.
What if I move UP and the projectile enters the frame, destroying it? Let me try moving UP from the current position.
Current: player at rows 37-39, cols 20-23; projectile at rows 33-34, cols 21-22
If I move UP, player goes to rows 33-35. But the projectile is at rows 33-34, so they would overlap. Previously, moving UP from rows 37-39 didn't work because the projectile blocked.
Wait, but the player is now at cols 20-23, and the projectile is at cols 21-22. Target B is at cols 16-19. So I'm not aligned with target B anymore.
Let me go back to aligning with target B first, then figure out how to destroy it.
Actually, let me reconsider. Maybe the key is to get the projectile to overlap with the target frame. If I align with target B (cols 16-19) and move UP, the projectile would enter the frame at rows 28-31. Maybe that's how targets are destroyed - by having the projectile collide with them.
Let me go LEFT to realign with target B, then try moving UP.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model: Blue projectile follows player, always 4 rows above and centered. Player at cols 20-23, need to go back to cols 16-19 to align with target B. Projectile may need to collide with target frame to destroy it.
Plan: Move LEFT to realign with target B, then try UP to push projectile into the frame.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Move LEFT to realign with target B, then try UP
predict(expect_change=True, note='LEFT realigns with target B')
action(['LEFT'])

seg = current_frame.segmentation
nodes = seg['nodes']
for n in nodes:
    if n['color'] == 'N':
        b = n['boundary']
        rows = [p[0] for p in b]
        cols = [p[1] for p in b]
        print(f"Player: rows [{min(rows)}-{max(rows)}], cols [{min(cols)}-{max(cols)}]")
    if n['color'] == 'b' and n['pixels'] == 4:
        b = n['boundary']
        rows = [p[0] for p in b]
        cols = [p[1] for p in b]
        print(f"Blue: rows [{min(rows)}-{max(rows)}], cols [{min(cols)}-{max(cols)}]")
    if n['color'] == 'c' and n['pixels'] == 12:
        b = n['boundary']
        rows = [p[0] for p in b]
        cols = [p[1] for p in b]
        print(f"Frame: rows [{min(rows)}-{max(rows)}], cols [{min(cols)}-{max(cols)}]")

# Now try UP to see if projectile enters frame
predict(expect_change=True, note='UP may push projectile into target B frame')
action(['UP'])

seg = current_frame.segmentation
nodes = seg['nodes']
print(f"\nAfter UP:")
for n in nodes:
    if n['color'] == 'N':
        b = n['boundary']
        rows = [p[0] for p in b]
        cols = [p[1] for p in b]
        print(f"Player: rows [{min(rows)}-{max(rows)}], cols [{min(cols)}-{max(cols)}]")
    if n['color'] == 'b' and n['pixels'] == 4:
        b = n['boundary']
        rows = [p[0] for p in b]
        cols = [p[1] for p in b]
        print(f"Blue: rows [{min(rows)}-{max(rows)}], cols [{min(cols)}-{max(cols)}]")
    if n['color'] == 'c' and n['pixels'] == 12:
        b = n['boundary']
        rows = [p[0] for p in b]
        cols = [p[1] for p in b]
        print(f"Frame: rows [{min(rows)}-{max(rows)}], cols [{min(cols)}-{max(cols)}]")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
